straight_beat.name: Phil Antonelli
straight_beat.column: The Guardians handed the Royals a 10-2 spanking yesterday, a win that feels less like a breakthrough and more like a reminder of what’s possible when the pieces align. The ledger shows they’re 8-6, still in the thick of the AL Central race, but the gap to the division leaders is three games. That

optimist.name: Peggy Vukovich
optimist.column:
The Guardians lost yesterday, but let’s not pretend that loss is the end of the story. No, the Tribe never dies, shh. Not when there’s still 100 games left, not when the heart of this team is still beating louder than the Royals’ drum. You saw it in the way José Ramírez chased every pitch, the way his bat stayed hot even when the scoreboard said otherwise. This is the year. I felt it during the pierogi race.  

Sure, the Royals handed them a 10-2 drubbing, but let’s not forget what happened in the seventh inning. That solo home run by Owen Miller? That

pessimist.name: Stan Kowalke
pessimist.column:
The Guardians lost again. Not just lost, but lost in a way that makes you want to throw your coffee across the room. Kansas City, of all teams, handed us our eighth straight defeat, and now we’re 8-5, clinging to the AL Central like a lifeline we’re not sure we want to hold onto. Dolan’s ceiling? Let’s not talk about that. Let’s talk about how we’re still in this race, and how that’s a damn miracle.  

The Royals didn’t just beat us; they exposed us. Their pitching staff, which looked like it might implode last season, has found its groove. Meanwhile, our own hurlers? They’re either injured, struggling, or both. The bullpen, that supposed fortress, looked like a sieve. You know what’s funny? We’ve had the same bullpen issues for years, and yet Dolan keeps signing free agents who can’t throw a strike. Maybe the ceiling is just a fancy word for “disaster in disguise.”  

Let’s pivot to the manager. Is there a reason why we’re still in this race? Because the front office is too busy drafting prospects to realize we’re already in a hole? Greg Budzinski’s lineup yesterday was a masterclass in “let’s just hit the ball into the gap.” We had runners in scoring position, and we couldn’t capitalize. It’s not just the bats; it’s the way we’re managing the game. You can’t win a playoff race by playing .500 ball. You
